narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your overall profile and estimated a 69% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most strongly on your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 performance, where you ranked in the bottom decile (1 out of 10) compared to the average student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. That low Year 3 decile was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance over time. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom (1 out of 10 versus the average 5.71), reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting early struggles that carried weight in the model's eyes. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success because it's stronger than what most applicants bring. Other aspects, like your low family income bracket and an LSAT score of 27.50—well below the 36.70 average—played supporting roles, but it was those early and later school deciles that dominated the decision. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often rank in higher deciles throughout school, your position in the bottom tiers set you apart in a way the model couldn't overlook. Full-time enrollment plans, common for over 90% of applicants, didn't sway things much here. While this rejection stings, especially with your above-average undergrad record, it highlights where the model saw risks in your trajectory. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score significantly, seeking targeted tutoring to improve upper-year grades if possible, or building a stronger narrative in reapplications about how you've addressed those early academic hurdles—many students turn things around and succeed on later bar attempts.
